english: Turkish fried eggplant with tomato sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Saksuka exists; closest defensible anchor is eggplant food product (FOODON_03311737) given eggplant is the primary ingredient. Broader term used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for the composite dish Saksuka. Closest defensible code is A00GJ (Aubergine/eggplant, cooked) as eggplant is the dominant ingredient. Composite dish with tomato sauce and peppers not individually coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Saksuka or a sufficiently close Turkish vegetable mezze dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Saksuka is one small plate of approximately 200 grams, providing around 220 kcal, suitable as a mezze appetizer or vegetable side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11210) and Peppers, sweet, red, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11821); composite estimate for Saksuka
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Saksuka exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked eggplant, cooked red bell peppers, tomato sauce, garlic, and olive oil in proportions consistent with a standard Saksuka recipe (approximately 40% eggplant, 20% peppers, 25% tomato sauce, 10% olive oil, 5% garlic/onion/herbs). Input record values (energy 110 kcal, protein 2.0g, fat 9.0g, carbohydrate 6.0g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 175mg/100g is plausible for a home-style preparation with added salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for cooked eggplant and mixed vegetable dish in tomato sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Saksuka found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous entries: cooked eggplant GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database), cooked bell peppers GI ~15, tomato-based sauce GI ~35, and olive oil contribution (negligible GI effect). Weighted composite estimate for the dish yields approximately GI 35. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 6.0g carbohydrate × 200/100) / 100 = 4.2g, classified as low GL. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 175mg/100g is below the 200mg/100g threshold; potassium-rich vegetables support DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Saksuka is suitable for hypertension management at standard serving sizes; patients should request reduced added salt during preparation to keep sodium below 150mg/100g, and the potassium content (~300mg/100g from eggplant, peppers, and tomatoes) actively supports blood pressure reduction per DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6.0g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.5g/100g), and estimated low GI (~35) from vegetable-dominant composition with olive oil
type2_diabetes.guidance: Saksuka is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate load, high fibre from eggplant and peppers, and low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35; patients should avoid pairing with large portions of refined carbohydrates such as white rice or bread to prevent overall meal glycaemic load from rising.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat low at 1.25g/100g; olive oil provides beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids; cholesterol 0mg/100g; antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saksuka is cardioprotective when prepared with olive oil in moderate quantities; the low saturated fat (1.25g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant content from tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant support cardiovascular health, while the monounsaturated fat profile from olive oil aligns with Mediterranean dietary guidelines for CVD risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, bell peppers, tomatoes) contain solanine alkaloids which may aggravate joint inflammation in solanine-sensitive RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saksuka is not recommended as a general dish for rheumatoid arthritis patients sensitive to nightshade vegetables; those with confirmed solanine sensitivity should avoid or substitute eggplant, peppers, and tomatoes with non-nightshade alternatives such as zucchini or mushrooms, while those with confirmed nightshade tolerance may benefit from the anti-inflammatory oleocanthal in olive oil and vitamin C (15mg/100g) present in the dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saksuka (Turkish: Şakşuka) is a traditional Turkish mezze appetizer and side dish originating from Anatolia, widely served across Turkey and in Turkish restaurants globally. The dish is composed primarily of fried or sautéed eggplant (aubergine) and bell peppers, simmered in a rich tomato and garlic sauce with olive oil, onions, and fresh herbs. The result is a tender, silky vegetable preparation with a deep umami-tomato flavour, mild sweetness from the peppers, and a slightly smoky character from the fried eggplant. Saksuka is typically served at room temperature or slightly warm as part of a mezze spread, alongside dishes such as hummus, cacık, and stuffed vine leaves. Nutritionally, Saksuka is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable dish providing approximately 110 kcal per 100g, with 9g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), 2g protein, 6g carbohydrate, and 2.5g dietary fibre. Sodium is approximately 175mg/100g. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Its low estimated glycemic index (~35) and high fibre content make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension management. The nightshade-dominant composition (eggplant, peppers, tomatoes) warrants caution for rheumatoid arthritis patients sensitive to solanine. Vitamin C content (~15mg/100g) and potassium (~300mg/100g) contribute to its antioxidant and DASH-diet-compatible nutritional profile.
common_user_queries: Is saksuka good for high blood pressure?
Turkish appetizer with eggplant and peppers

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Saksuka found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked eggplant, cooked bell peppers, tomato sauce, and olive oil in standard Saksuka proportions. Input record values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for component vegetables. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish estimated from approved component sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked eggplant NDB 11210, cooked red bell peppers NDB 11821, tomato sauce, olive oil, garlic) in proportions consistent with a standard Saksuka recipe. No exact approved-source entry for Saksuka as a composite dish exists. Input record values retained as consistent with the composite estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.5g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.0g) are estimated from olive oil contribution (~10g olive oil per 100g dish); these are not directly reported in any approved source for this dish and are marked as estimated. 3. potassium_mg (300mg/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central values for cooked eggplant (~123mg/100g), cooked bell peppers (~166mg/100g), and tomato sauce (~217mg/100g) in standard proportions; not directly measured for this dish. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for Saksuka as a composite dish not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source; component-level data exists but composite dish values are not published. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero as this is an all-plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed zero as this dish contains no vitamin D sources (no fish, eggs, or fortified dairy). 6. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked eggplant and mixed vegetable dishes in tomato sauce; no direct GI measurement for Saksuka found. 7. Saksuka should not be confused with Shakshuka (eggs poached in tomato sauce); they are distinct dishes despite phonetic similarity. 8. country_of_origin in input record was listed as Singapore, which reflects the data source context (GrabFood Singapore listing); the dish origin is Turkey and this has been corrected in region_of_origin.
